 {"id":"asupersync-xrc.2.1","title":"Implement region heap allocator + quiescent reclamation","description":"# Region Heap Allocator + Quiescent Reclamation

## Purpose
Enable safe parallel tasks by allocating captured data in a region-owned heap that is reclaimed only when the region closes to quiescence.

This is the memory backbone for the “task tier.”

## Requirements
- Region heap lifetime = region lifetime.
- Reclamation only after:
  - all child tasks terminal
  - all finalizers complete
  - all obligations resolved

## Design Notes
- Start with a simple allocator design:
  - bump allocator per region for fast-path
  - fallback to global allocator when necessary
- Determinism: allocation addresses must not be used as observable identifiers.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Region heap allocations remain valid for all tasks owned by the region.
- Region heap is reclaimed on region close without leaks.

## Testing
- Allocate values in region heap, spawn tasks that read them, close region, ensure memory is reclaimed (via debug counters, not UB).

 {"id":"asupersync-xrc.2.2","title":"Implement RRef<'r, T> (region-owned Send reference)","description":"# RRef<'r, T> (Region-Owned Reference)

## Purpose
Provide a way for migrating (`Send`) tasks to reference data allocated in the region heap safely.

## Requirements
- `RRef<'r, T>` ties to region lifetime `'r`.
- `RRef<'r, T>` can be `Send`/`Sync` when `T` is.
- No unsafe code in core (if possible). If unavoidable, redesign.

## Design Sketch
- Internally represent as:
  - `Arc<RegionHeap>` + offset/index into heap
  - OR `Arc<T>` allocated via region heap wrapper (but `Arc` implies refcount overhead)

Given performance goals, prefer region heap + offset, but we must reconcile with `unsafe` prohibition.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Users can spawn Send tasks that capture `RRef`s without lifetime violations.
- Region close guarantees memory validity until tasks are done.

## Testing
- Compile-time tests for trait bounds (`Send`/`Sync` conditions).
- Runtime tests: create `RRef`, spawn tasks across workers, validate reads.

 {"id":"asupersync-xrc.2.3","title":"Define Send task tier API and soundness rules","description":"# Send Task Tier API + Soundness Rules

## Purpose
Make the “soundness frontier” explicit:
- Phase 0 supports single-thread fibers that can borrow.
- Phase 1 adds Send tasks that may migrate across workers.

This task defines the API and rules for spawning Send tasks.

## Rules to Encode
- A migrating task must be `Send`.
- Captured data must be safe across threads:
  - either `'static`
  - or allocated in region heap and referenced via `RRef<'r, T>`.
- Cancellation and obligations semantics are identical to fibers.

## API Sketch
```rust
impl<'r> Scope<'r> {
    pub fn spawn_task<T: Send + 'r>(...) -> JoinHandle<'r, T>;
}
```

We may also model capabilities:
- `FiberCap` vs `TaskCap`

## Acceptance Criteria
- API prevents accidental unsound captures.
- Lab runtime can simulate this tier deterministically.

## Testing
- Compile-fail tests for borrowing captures in Send tasks.
- Runtime tests for cross-thread execution.

-{"id":"asupersync-xrc.3","title":"Phase 1: Deterministic Parallel Lab Simulation","description":"# Phase 1: Deterministic Parallel Lab Simulation

## Purpose
Preserve deterministic testing after adding parallelism.

Even if production runtime uses real threads and OS scheduling, the lab runtime must be able to:
- model multiple workers deterministically
- replay a chosen interleaving
- provide stable traces

## Plan-of-Record
- Represent “parallelism” in lab as a deterministic interleaving of worker steps controlled by seed/schedule.
- Expose explicit schedule control to Phase 5 DPOR tooling.

## Acceptance Criteria
- For a given seed and config, lab parallel runs produce identical traces.
- Replay works across parallel configurations.

## Testing
- Same scenario run twice in parallel lab config yields identical trace.
- Cross-check: single-thread vs parallel-lab produce equivalent outcomes (where appropriate).

 {"id":"asupersync-xrc.3.1","title":"Extend lab runtime to model N workers deterministically","description":"# Deterministic N-Worker Lab Runtime Model

## Purpose
Phase 0 lab runtime is single-threaded. Phase 1 needs a deterministic model of multiple workers.

## Plan-of-Record
- Represent the runtime as a set of worker states.
- Each “step” chooses:
  - which worker runs next
  - which task that worker polls (respecting lane priorities)
- Choice is controlled by:
  - explicit seed
  - optional externally-provided schedule (Phase 5)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Same seed/config produces identical traces.
- A captured schedule can be replayed.

## Testing
- Run identical parallel scenarios twice and compare traces.
- Stress: many tasks with steals; determinism must still hold.

 {"id":"asupersync-xrc.4","title":"Phase 1: Parallel Verification Suite","description":"# Phase 1: Parallel Verification Suite

## Purpose
Extend Phase 0’s verification (oracles, unit tests, E2E scenarios, benchmarks) to cover the parallel scheduler and region heap.

## Required Additions
- Stress tests for work-stealing correctness
- Determinism tests for parallel lab simulation
- Region heap safety tests
- Performance baselines for parallel spawn/scheduling overhead

## Acceptance Criteria
- All Phase 0 tests still pass.
- New parallel tests cover:
  - no duplicate polls
  - no lost tasks
  - cancellation drains under contention
  - region close quiescence under parallel scheduling

 {"id":"asupersync-xrc.4.1","title":"Add parallel stress tests (work stealing, cancellation, quiescence)","description":"# Parallel Stress Tests

## Purpose
Validate Phase 1 under load:
- work-stealing correctness
- cancellation drain under contention
- region close quiescence with migrated tasks

## Scenarios
- Many short tasks; ensure all complete (no duplicates/no loss).
- Many long tasks; cancel parent region; ensure drain completes and tasks terminal.
- Mix timers + steals.

## Logging / Debuggability
On failure, dump:
- trace
- per-worker queue snapshots
- first invariant violation evidence

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests pass deterministically under lab simulation.
- Failures are reproducible via seed and/or saved schedule.
